report.summary: 44d88612fea8a8f36de82e1278abb02f is the MD5 hash of the EICAR antivirus test file — a standardised, harmless 68-byte string that every antivirus engine is required to detect, used globally to verify that AV software is functioning correctly. While MalwareBazaar classifies it as a known file and AV engines flag it as 'malicious', this is by design and reflects the test file's purpose, not actual malicious intent. Encountering this hash in your environment most likely indicates AV software testing, security tooling validation, or developer unit tests — not an actual infection.
key_findings: MalwareBazaar identifies this as the EICAR standard antivirus test file — not real malware, but a harmless 68-byte compliance test string
All network-based sources (GreyNoise, AbuseIPDB, Shodan, CIRCL pDNS, VT Passive DNS) return not_applicable — there are no network IOCs associated with this hash
3 OTX pulses are research/educational entries documenting the EICAR test file, not incident reports
GitHub: 214 repositories reference this hash, predominantly in security tools, test suites, and AV validation frameworks
The SHA256 (275a021b...) and SHA1 (3395856c...) are equally well-known test vectors — their presence in any threat feed is expected and benign
recommended_actions: No action required — EICAR is a harmless test file by design
If this hash appeared in a security alert, verify your AV/EDR is configured to suppress EICAR detections in known testing contexts
If you are unsure why this hash appeared in your environment, check which process created or downloaded the file — this may reveal a misconfigured security tool or developer testing
top_supporting_evidence: MalwareBazaar signature is 'EICAR Test File' — an internationally standardised test vector, not a real malware family
All 9 network-based sources (GreyNoise, AbuseIPDB, Shodan, pDNS, URLhaus) return not_applicable — a real malware sample would have network IOCs
214 GitHub repositories reference this hash, the vast majority in security tooling, AV test suites, and sandbox validation frameworks
top_conflicting_evidence: MalwareBazaar labels the file type as 'Win32 EXE', which is technically accurate for the EICAR format but could cause automated systems to treat this as a real executable threat without proper context
